title: Industrial Design Services

narrative.executive_view: Industrial design services present high workflow exposure but weak ownership of the resulting value. Generative tools can compress visual exploration, concept communication, research synthesis, presentation material, and administrative production; the defensible residue is user understanding, design direction, engineering judgment, prototype iteration, and client trust. The supplied labor share and occupation mix are unusually unreliable for this narrow code, so the calculated labor opportunity should be treated as an upper-bound signal rather than a deal thesis. The more fundamental concern is that a corporate client can apply the same tools internally or re-bid a fixed-scope engagement [S1][S2].
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The best evidence points to AI as a rapidly adopted collaborator in industrial-design workflows rather than a finished autonomous product-development system. Interviews with 20 experienced industrial designers report bottom-up adoption driven by speed pressure, while also identifying loss of control over micro-decisions as a risk [S1]. The most exposed tasks are mood boards, variants, rendering, research summaries, proposals, schedules, and routine documentation. Physical prototypes, manufacturability trade-offs, field research, client workshops, and responsibility for a production-ready choice still require humans and cross-functional work; an IDSA-listed product-development consultancy explicitly combines research, industrial design, engineering, testing, and in-house prototypes [S4].
narrative.value_capture: Engagement economics create a short-lived retention window, not a durable moat. A product-design consultancy describes fixed fees for defined, mostly known work but describes fee-for-service and staged billing as more common for exploratory outsourced physical-product work [S3]. A studio can initially finish a fixed-price milestone with fewer hours, but renewal, change orders, and the next procurement cycle expose the new productivity to re-scoping and price competition. The capture estimates therefore assume only partial retention and a low studio moat; they do not use the physical prototype residue to inflate capture.
narrative.adoption_timing: Effective use is below 50% of the selected task share but is likely to reach that point faster than in a regulated or field-service industry. A Foundation Capital and Designer Fund survey of nearly 400 designers found grassroots adoption, with startups adopting AI twice as fast as large firms, while the industrial-design interviews also describe rapid workflow adoption [S1][S2]. Those are not a representative census of US lower-middle-market 541420 studios, so the selected current adoption and three-and-a-half-year midpoint remain cautious estimates. Organizational IP, client confidentiality, CAD interoperability, and quality control are likely to slow conversion from tool use to sustained labor displacement.
narrative.consolidation_economics: This is not a demonstrated PE roll-up market. McKinsey acquired LUNAR in 2015 to add design execution to an advisory practice, and a 2024 UK transaction combined Fen Technology with Wright Design to integrate industrial and mechanical design into a broader product-development offer [S5][S6]. Those are capability acquisitions, not proof that stand-alone studios clear conventional multiple-arbitrage exits. General middle-market valuation data is materially broader than this industry, so the small selected multiple spread assumes only a modest improvement if a buyer reduces founder dependence and adds engineering or prototyping breadth [S7].
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED rather than melting immediately. Complex products still require external help where a client lacks research, engineering, prototype, or manufacturing capability; Delve's described service stack illustrates that broader physical-product-development offering [S4]. But no reviewed evidence establishes a regulatory or licensing gate that forces customers to retain an outside industrial-design studio. AI makes visual and early-concept work easier to internalize, and mature clients can combine an in-house team with outside specialists. A buyer should underwrite only the differentiated physical and strategic residue, not broad outsourced concept-production volume.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive uncertainty is market boundary. The supplied 4-digit role mix is dominated by interior and graphic designers from sibling codes and omits Commercial and Industrial Designers, while the residual must stand in for the actual core product-design and engineering labor. The supplied 0.9177 labor share is also flagged as implausibly high by its own vintage construction. There is no reviewed 541420 owner-age survey, succession-shortage evidence, US studio multiple database, or US outsourcing-versus-insourcing time series. These evidence gaps make this a low-confidence underwriting case even though AI exposure is directionally clear.

sources.title: A survey of generative AI adoption amongst industrial design experts
sources.what_it_supports: A 2026 Design Research Society paper based on interviews with 20 experienced industrial designers describes rapid bottom-up adoption under speed pressure and risks from cognitive offloading and loss of control over design micro-decisions.

sources.title: The State of AI in Design: What we learned from 400+ designers
sources.what_it_supports: A Foundation Capital and Designer Fund survey of nearly 400 designers reports grassroots AI adoption, 96% self-taught tool discovery, and faster adoption at startups than large firms. Its population is broad design, not industrial-design consultancies.

sources.title: How Much Do Product Design Firms Charge?
sources.what_it_supports: A product-design consultancy describes fixed-price, fee-for-service, and fee-plus-equity models, including fixed fees for defined work and staged or hourly arrangements for exploratory outsourced physical-product development.

sources.title: Delve - Industrial Designers Society of America
sources.what_it_supports: An IDSA design-firm directory entry describes a US product-development consultancy combining user research, industrial design, human factors, engineering, prototyping, testing, and in-house prototype development. It is an example, not an industry census.

sources.title: Landing LUNAR
sources.what_it_supports: McKinsey's 2015 announcement says its acquisition of industrial-design firm LUNAR added design execution to its product-development advisory practice. Terms were undisclosed and the transaction is historical.

sources.title: Fen Technology announces the acquisition of Wright Design
sources.what_it_supports: A 2024 UK announcement describes Fen Technology's acquisition of Wright Design, an industrial and mechanical design consultancy, to integrate complementary product-development capabilities.

sources.title: Middle Market M&A Valuations Index
sources.what_it_supports: A broad 2025 US middle-market report cites 9.8x average valuations across its covered markets and surveyed-bankers' expected typical and premium 2026 multiples of 6.8x and 9.8x. It is not an industrial-design-studio comp set.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541420 ($2,501,498,861) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541420 ($2,906,073,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $233,495 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541420); class <5: 1,208 firms, avg revenue/firm $356,066 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $55,724 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 177 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,730,514 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $270,825 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 94 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,101,245 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $485,345 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 85 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,402,424 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,627,979 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,169,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,068,948 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,040,567 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,414,849 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 131
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Fractions credit AI for visual ideation, rendering, research synthesis, routine documentation, proposals, scheduling, and administrative production, while withholding credit for embodied prototypes, user research, client leadership, and design-for-manufacture judgment. The core occupation is absent from the supplied 4-digit mix, so the residual uses a cautious blended judgment rather than treating sibling-code graphics roles as a direct industrial-design measurement.
caveats: The supplied 4-digit 5414 role mix is contaminated by sibling-code interior and graphic design and omits Commercial and Industrial Designers.
The 0.3739 residual wage share carries the core product-design, engineering, prototype, and other unlisted work; its fraction is a broad hypothesis.
S1 is a qualitative 20-person interview study and S2 is broad design research, neither a representative 541420 task-time survey.
High task exposure does not assume a studio can retain the savings; pass-through is scored only in pi_dist and pi_moat.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Interior Designers are a sibling-code contamination but have exposed visualization, layout, variants, and documentation work; site context, client choice, and implementation coordination remain human.
Graphic Designers have highly exposed production graphics, image variants, pitch assets, and routine content work, while direction, brand judgment, and client review remain.
Operations managers retain client leadership, hiring, creative accountability, and commercial judgment; reporting, planning, and internal coordination are addressable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proposal drafts, account research, and follow-up can be automated, but relationship selling and discovery of an ambiguous product problem remain human.
Schedules, risk logs, status reporting, and budget tracking are addressable; trade-off arbitration across client, engineering, and creative work remains.
General office records, invoice support, routine data movement, and correspondence are highly addressable, subject to controlled client records and review.

breakdown_by_role.role: Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Material movement and hands-on support are physical tasks; only simple labels, routing, and associated documentation are addressable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Market-research synthesis, case-study drafting, campaign variants, and routine analysis are exposed, while market judgment and client positioning remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Administrative coordination, contract paperwork preparation, calendaring, and invoicing support are highly addressable, with supervisory and exception work retained.
Art Directors can automate visual exploration and production variants, but retain aesthetic strategy, critique, client facilitation, and final direction.

The residual is expected to include core industrial/product designers, engineering, model-making, research, and production-support roles. It receives credit for CAD, rendering, specifications, and analysis support but not for prototyping, field research, manufacturability, or final product judgment.

pi_dist.rationale: A studio can retain some savings within an already signed fixed-price or staged milestone, but exploratory work is commonly fee-for-service and later milestones, renewal, scope definition, and procurement expose savings to re-pricing.
caveats: S3 is one Canadian consultancy's commercial explanation, not a US industry pricing survey.
This selection addresses savings pass-through only; it does not assume that external demand survives AI.

pi_moat.rationale: A broad integrated studio can retain some advantage through research, engineering, prototypes, and client knowledge, but a typical small studio has no reviewed licensing gate and faces client insourcing, founder dependence, and comparable AI tools.
caveats: S4 describes a larger, broad-service example and should not be generalized to all 541420 firms.
The same founder dependence also depresses exit multiples, but the distinct channel here is current competitive retention of operating savings.

t50_years.rationale: The midpoint reflects fast individual tool adoption in design but slower conversion into controlled, client-approved, CAD-connected, and repeatedly labor-saving studio workflows. It is not a forecast of full autonomous product development.
caveats: S1 is qualitative and S2 is broad design research with a startup-heavy adoption finding, not a representative 541420 adoption curve.
IP policy, client confidentiality, CAD interoperability, and training may make smaller studios slower or faster than the selected midpoint.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Twenty percent represents effective adoption against the selected replaceable task share, not survey respondents who have tried an AI tool. The evidence supports rapid grassroots use and uneven organizational integration, so the estimate remains well below 50%.
caveats: Neither source measures US lower-middle-market industrial-design task displacement.
Individual designer use should not be equated with a firm realizing headcount or contractor-cost reductions.

historical_analogs.value: The relevant analog is adoption of digital production tools in a creative workflow: individual experimentation precedes common team standards, client acceptance, and reliable commercial delivery, so use grows faster than captured labor savings.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources describe bottom-up, largely self-taught AI adoption and collaboration friction, supporting a staged workflow-adoption analogy rather than a quantified historical industrial-design time series.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Individual use can spread within 1-2 years; integrated labor savings can take 2-6 years
caveats: This is a qualitative analogy, not a measured history of CAD, rendering, or industrial-design employment.
Tool quality may advance faster than client approval, organizational policy, or product-liability acceptance.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No reviewed source measured owners over 60 for US 541420 firms or the supplied EBITDA band. The 25% selection is a deliberately broad planning assumption, not an observed demographic statistic.
caveats: Founder age and ownership are not inferable from professional-designer demographics or general small-business surveys.
This input needs a code- and size-specific owner survey before it can support acquisition sourcing claims.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reviewed source documented a 541420 succession shortage. The stricter selection is false rather than treating founder branding or a general demographic assumption as proof.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: true/false; selected false pending direct evidence
caveats: A shortage may exist locally, but it was not evidenced for this score input.

active_consolidators.rationale: One recent, directly reviewed capability acquisition is selected as a verified floor: Fen Technology acquired Wright Design in 2024. It demonstrates a strategic buyer for integrated product-development capability, not an active US PE roll-up market.
caveats: S6 is a UK transaction and not a count of US 541420 buyers.
Historic McKinsey-LUNAR is recorded as market precedent but is too old to increase the active-buyer count.

buy_mult.rationale: A 5.5x entry estimate is deliberately below broad surveyed-bankers' 6.8x typical middle-market expectation because a founder-led industrial-design studio has high client and key-person concentration, weak evidence of recurring revenue, and no direct comp database in the reviewed evidence.
caveats: S7 is broad middle-market M&A reporting, not an industrial-design or sub-$10M EBITDA transaction set.
Some studios may transact on adjusted EBITDA, revenue, acqui-hire, earnout, or talent-retention terms that are poorly represented by a simple EBITDA multiple.

exit_mult.rationale: A modest exit uplift assumes an acquirer has added engineering/prototype breadth, diversified customers, and reduced founder dependence. The selected multiple remains below broad typical middle-market expectations because reviewed design transactions are capability acquisitions with undisclosed terms, not comparable roll-up exits.
caveats: S5 is a 2015 acquisition and S6 is a 2024 UK transaction; neither discloses a usable multiple.
Multiple expansion is confined to this input and is not used to justify capture or terminal demand.

pricing_structure.value: Mix of defined fixed-price phases, exploratory fee-for-service or staged work, and occasional fee-plus-equity or royalty arrangements; the next scope definition, milestone, or renewal is the primary repricing point for AI productivity.
pricing_structure.rationale: The described product-design pricing models directly distinguish fixed defined work from exploratory hourly or staged work, which creates different timing for retention versus pass-through of AI savings.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Project-specific mix from mostly fixed defined phases to mostly staged exploratory fee-for-service work
caveats: S3 is a single firm's commercial explanation in Canada and may not represent US lower-middle-market 541420 studios.
Pricing differs materially by product complexity, IP ownership, client procurement rules, and whether engineering/prototyping is bundled.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The task assessment gives substantial credit to visual and documentation work, but adoption remains below 50% and the timing selection allows for the lag from individual use to integrated studio workflows. It does not imply immediate autonomous product development.

terminal_value.justification: External demand persists for differentiated research, engineering, prototypes, and product-development execution, but commoditized concepts and visual production face direct AI substitution and can be internalized by clients without a reviewed regulatory gate.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No external values replaced the supplied labor share, role weights, total firms, or target-band count. Unobserved inputs are marked estimates, and broader, foreign, historical, or firm-specific evidence is disclosed rather than portrayed as a US 541420 measurement.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Fixed-scope retention and repricing pressure are scored in pi_dist and pi_moat. External-demand risk from client insourcing and AI-enabled concept production is scored only in terminal demand, not double-counted as a labor replacement or multiple claim.

dataset_mismatch.description: The deterministic labor share combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and is unusually high at 0.9177. The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5414, is dominated by sibling-code interior and graphic designers, and omits the core Commercial and Industrial Designers occupation. The target-band estimate also contains a size-class revenue inversion.
sensitivity: A different vintage or revenue basis could materially lower labor share and reduce the labor-value factor.
A 541420-specific occupation mix could materially change AI task weighting because core industrial designers sit in the residual rather than the listed roles.
The small target pool and anomalous 500+ receipts make the deterministic target-band count sensitive to margin and classification assumptions.

subsegments: product development with mechanical and electrical engineering
market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Physical industrial and consumer-product design, selected from the six-digit title but not directly represented by the supplied role mix
market_boundary.selection_basis: The code title points to industrial design, while the supplied 4-digit occupation data includes adjacent design codes. UX/digital work, graphic-led studios, interior design, and engineering-heavy product development may be classified elsewhere or blended into firms, so cross-industry design evidence is used only with explicit scope limits.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Fast tool experimentation may not become durable workflow savings at the same pace, so the adoption factor is highly uncertain.
The 4-digit role contamination and large residual make the labor-value factor especially sensitive to a code-specific occupation mix.
Founder dependence, client insourcing, and whether the firm owns physical product-development capability determine retained savings.
No reviewed direct comp set supports conventional multiple expansion for standalone industrial-design studios.

reviewer_note: Research was conducted only for 541420. The packet deliberately treats role mix, labor share, ownership age, succession, pricing, and multiple support as weak where code-specific evidence was not found; no claim of a PE roll-up market is made.
